comment_text.sn35.247:1.5: Manu 9.256–260 describes various “thorns” (i.e. “pests”) as thieves, etc. Kauṭilya’s Arthaśāstra devotes the entirety of book 4 to removing thorns to the realm.
comment_text.sn35.247:3.1: Each of the six animals is connected to the corresponding sense in some way.
comment_text.sn35.247:3.2: Ahi (“snake”) puns with akkhi (“eye”). Also, the cobra is famed for the false eyes on its hood.
comment_text.sn35.247:3.3: Susumāra (literally “child-killer”) may be either the Gangetic dolphin (MN 129:21.3) or the crocodile (Cp 27:1.3). Here it is probably the dolphin, which has residual eyesight and primarily relies on echolocation. This association may be reinforced by the blowhole appearing like a prominent ear.
comment_text.sn35.247:3.4: A bird is notable for its beaked “nose”.
comment_text.sn35.247:3.5: Dogs are notoriously ravenous, thus standing for the tongue.
comment_text.sn35.247:3.10: The termite mound is said to be the body (MN 23:4.1), to which the eye is attracted. | The dolphin follows its ear. | The bird flies to the sky like someone sniffing the air. | The dog goes to the village where the food is. | The jackal feeds on discarded bodies. | The monkey leaps from branch to branch in the jungle (SN 12.61:4.2).

root_text.sn35.247:1.1: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso arugatto pakkagatto saravanaṁ paviseyya.
root_text.sn35.247:1.2: Tassa kusakaṇṭakā ceva pāde vijjheyyuṁ, sarapattāni ca gattāni vilekheyyuṁ.
root_text.sn35.247:1.3: Evañhi so, bhikkhave, puriso bhiyyoso mattāya tatonidānaṁ dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvediyetha.
root_text.sn35.247:1.4: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, idhekacco bhikkhu gāmagato vā araññagato vā labhati vattāraṁ:
root_text.sn35.247:1.6: Taṁ kaṇṭakoti iti viditvā saṁvaro ca asaṁvaro ca veditabbo.
root_text.sn35.247:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā piyarūpe rūpe adhimuccati, appiyarūpe rūpe byāpajjati, anupaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati parittacetaso.
root_text.sn35.247:2.3: Tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti, yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.247:2.8: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya piyarūpe dhamme adhimuccati, appiyarūpe dhamme byāpajjati, anupaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati parittacetaso,
root_text.sn35.247:2.9: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti, yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.247:3.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso chappāṇake gahetvā nānāvisaye nānāgocare daḷhāya rajjuyā bandheyya.
root_text.sn35.247:3.8: Daḷhāya rajjuyā bandhitvā majjhe gaṇṭhiṁ karitvā ossajjeyya.
root_text.sn35.247:3.9: Atha kho, te, bhikkhave, chappāṇakā nānāvisayā nānāgocarā sakaṁ sakaṁ gocaravisayaṁ āviñcheyyuṁ—
root_text.sn35.247:3.10: ahi āviñcheyya ‘vammikaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, susumāro āviñcheyya ‘udakaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, pakkhī āviñcheyya ‘ākāsaṁ ḍessāmī’ti, kukkuro āviñcheyya ‘gāmaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, siṅgālo āviñcheyya ‘sīvathikaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, makkaṭo āviñcheyya ‘vanaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti.
root_text.sn35.247:3.11: Yadā kho te, bhikkhave, chappāṇakā jhattā assu kilantā, atha kho yo nesaṁ pāṇakānaṁ balavataro assa tassa te anuvatteyyuṁ, anuvidhāyeyyuṁ vasaṁ gaccheyyuṁ.
root_text.sn35.247:3.12: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, yassa kassaci bhikkhuno kāyagatāsati abhāvitā abahulīkatā, taṁ cakkhu āviñchati manāpiyesu rūpesu, amanāpiyā rūpā paṭikūlā honti …pe…
root_text.sn35.247:3.13: mano āviñchati manāpiyesu dhammesu, amanāpiyā dhammā paṭikūlā honti.
root_text.sn35.247:4.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā piyarūpe rūpe nādhimuccati, appiyarūpe rūpe na byāpajjati, upaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati appamāṇacetaso,
root_text.sn35.247:4.3: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti …pe…
root_text.sn35.247:4.5: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya piyarūpe dhamme nādhimuccati, appiyarūpe dhamme na byāpajjati, upaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati appamāṇacetaso,
root_text.sn35.247:4.6: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.247:5.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso chappāṇake gahetvā nānāvisaye nānāgocare daḷhāya rajjuyā bandheyya.
root_text.sn35.247:5.8: Daḷhāya rajjuyā bandhitvā daḷhe khīle vā thambhe vā upanibandheyya.
root_text.sn35.247:5.9: Atha kho te, bhikkhave, chappāṇakā nānāvisayā nānāgocarā sakaṁ sakaṁ gocaravisayaṁ āviñcheyyuṁ—
root_text.sn35.247:5.10: ahi āviñcheyya ‘vammikaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, susumāro āviñcheyya ‘udakaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, pakkhī āviñcheyya ‘ākāsaṁ ḍessāmī’ti, kukkuro āviñcheyya ‘gāmaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, siṅgālo āviñcheyya ‘sīvathikaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti, makkaṭo āviñcheyya ‘vanaṁ pavekkhāmī’ti.
root_text.sn35.247:5.11: Yadā kho te, bhikkhave, chappāṇakā jhattā assu kilantā, atha tameva khīlaṁ vā thambhaṁ vā upatiṭṭheyyuṁ, upanisīdeyyuṁ, upanipajjeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn35.247:5.12: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, yassa kassaci bhikkhuno kāyagatāsati bhāvitā bahulīkatā, taṁ cakkhu nāviñchati manāpiyesu rūpesu, amanāpiyā rūpā nappaṭikūlā honti …pe… jivhā nāviñchati manāpiyesu rasesu …pe…
root_text.sn35.247:5.13: mano nāviñchati manāpiyesu dhammesu, amanāpiyā dhammā nappaṭikūlā honti.
root_text.sn35.247:6.1: ‘Daḷhe khīle vā thambhe vā’ti kho, bhikkhave, kāyagatāya satiyā etaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.247:6.3: ‘kāyagatā no sati bhāvitā bhavissati bahulīkatā yānīkatā vatthukatā anuṭṭhitā paricitā susamāraddhā’ti.

translation_text.sn35.247:1.1: “Mendicants, suppose a person with wounded and festering limbs was to enter a thicket of reeds.
translation_text.sn35.247:1.2: The kusa thorns would pierce their feet, and the reed leaves would scratch their limbs.
translation_text.sn35.247:1.3: And that would cause that person to experience even more pain and distress.
translation_text.sn35.247:1.4: In the same way, some mendicant goes to a village or a wilderness and gets scolded,
translation_text.sn35.247:1.5: ‘This venerable, acting like this, behaving like this, is a filthy village thorn.’
translation_text.sn35.247:1.6: Understanding that they’re a thorn, they should understand restraint and lack of restraint.
translation_text.sn35.247:2.2: Take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted.
translation_text.sn35.247:2.3: And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.247:2.4: When they hear a sound with their ears …
translation_text.sn35.247:2.5: When they smell an odor with their nose …
translation_text.sn35.247:2.6: When they taste a flavor with their tongue …
translation_text.sn35.247:2.7: When they feel a touch with their body …
translation_text.sn35.247:2.8: When they know an idea with their mind, if it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and a limited heart.
translation_text.sn35.247:2.9: And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.247:3.1: Suppose a person was to catch six animals, with diverse territories and feeding grounds, and tie them up with a strong rope.
translation_text.sn35.247:3.8: tie each up with a strong rope, then tie a knot in the middle and let them loose.
translation_text.sn35.247:3.9: Then those six animals with different domains and ranges would each pull towards their own domain and range.
translation_text.sn35.247:3.10: The snake would pull one way, thinking ‘I’m going into a termite mound!’ The dolphin would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the water!’ The bird would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m flying into the atmosphere!’ The dog would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the village!’ The jackal would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the charnel ground!’ The monkey would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the jungle!’
translation_text.sn35.247:3.11: When those six animals became exhausted and worn out, the strongest of them would get their way, and they’d all have to submit to their control.
translation_text.sn35.247:3.12: In the same way, when a mendicant has not developed or cultivated mindfulness of the body, their eye pulls towards pleasant sights, but is put off by unpleasant sights. Their ear … nose … tongue … body …
translation_text.sn35.247:3.13: mind pulls towards pleasant ideas, but is put off by unpleasant ideas.
translation_text.sn35.247:3.14: This is how someone is unrestrained.
translation_text.sn35.247:4.2: Take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart.
translation_text.sn35.247:4.3: And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.247:4.4: They hear a sound … smell an odor … taste a flavor … feel a touch …
translation_text.sn35.247:4.5: know an idea with their mind. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart.
translation_text.sn35.247:4.6: And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.247:5.1: Suppose a person was to catch six animals, with diverse territories and feeding grounds, and tie them up with a strong rope.
translation_text.sn35.247:5.8: tie each up with a strong rope, then tether them to a strong post or pillar.
translation_text.sn35.247:5.9: Then those six animals with different domains and ranges would each pull towards their own domain and range.
translation_text.sn35.247:5.10: The snake would pull one way, thinking ‘I’m going into a termite mound!’ The dolphin would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the water!’ The bird would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m flying into the atmosphere!’ The dog would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the village!’ The jackal would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the charnel ground!’ The monkey would pull another way, thinking ‘I’m going into the jungle!’
translation_text.sn35.247:5.11: When those six animals became exhausted and worn out, they’d stand or sit or lie down right by that post or pillar.
translation_text.sn35.247:5.12: In the same way, when a mendicant has developed and cultivated mindfulness of the body, their eye doesn’t pull towards pleasant sights, and isn’t put off by unpleasant sights. Their ear … nose … tongue … body …
translation_text.sn35.247:5.13: mind doesn’t pull towards pleasant ideas, and isn’t put off by unpleasant ideas.
translation_text.sn35.247:5.14: This is how someone is restrained.
translation_text.sn35.247:6.1: ‘A strong post or pillar’ is a term for mindfulness of the body.
translation_text.sn35.247:6.2: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn35.247:6.3: ‘We will develop mindfulness of the body. We’ll cultivate it, make it our vehicle and our basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’

variant_text.sn35.247:1.2: sarapattāni ca gattāni → sarapattāni pakkagattāni (sya-all, km); arupakkāni gattāni (pts1ed, mr)
variant_text.sn35.247:1.5: ayañca so → ayañca kho (pts1ed, mr); ayaṁ so (?)
